rationale: In the citing paper, Khan and Dhar (2006) is used in the Study 4 theorizing to ground the claim that indulgent consumption involves guilt and can be justified through prosocial behavior. That citation supports a specific moderator hypothesis about when donation promotions become more attractive relative to discounts. The paper also revisits the licensing idea in the discussion as prior evidence for altruism-based rationalization. This is more than a passing literature-list mention, even though the citing paper does not directly reproduce the original sequential licensing design.
relation_description: The citing paper applies Khan and Dhar's licensing/justification insight to a promotion-choice setting, arguing that donation tie-ins can offset guilt from indulgent purchases. It uses this logic to explain and test product-type differences in preference for donation versus discount promotions.
